learning_objectives: Describe the procurement management process from planning to closure
Perform make-or-buy analysis using quantitative and qualitative factors
readings: Garrett, Managing Complex Outsourced Projects, Ch. 1-3

lecture_notes.introduction: Procurement management governs the acquisition of goods and services from outside the project team. The decision of what to make internally versus buy externally is fundamental.
key_concepts: Procurement processes: plan, conduct, control, close
Make-or-buy analysis: compare internal cost (including overhead) vs. vendor cost (including management overhead)
Factors beyond cost: core competency, capacity, schedule, risk, intellectual property
Procurement management plan: types of contracts, roles, timelines, evaluation criteria
Procurement statement of work (SOW) vs. project scope statement
lecture_notes.demonstration: Performing a make-or-buy analysis for a software development component with cost and risk data

review.value: Buy when the work is not core competency, vendors have expertise/capacity, or internal resources are constrained
Describes the item to be procured in sufficient detail for sellers to determine if they can provide it

learning_objectives: Compare fixed-price, cost-reimbursable, and time-and-materials contracts
Select the appropriate contract type based on project risk profile
Understand incentive and award fee structures

lecture_notes.introduction: The contract type determines who bears the risk. Choosing the right type aligns vendor incentives with project objectives.
key_concepts: Fixed-Price (FP): firm price, seller bears cost risk; FFP, FPIF, FP-EPA variants
Cost-Reimbursable (CR): buyer pays actual costs plus fee; CPFF, CPIF, CPAF variants
Time and Materials (T&M): hybrid, pay for time at agreed rates; buyer bears most risk
Risk continuum: FFP (most risk on seller) to CPFF (most risk on buyer)
Incentive contracts: share savings or overruns based on a target cost
lecture_notes.demonstration: Calculating seller profit under FPIF and CPIF contracts with target costs, ceilings, and sharing ratios

review.value: Target cost + target fee + sharing ratio + ceiling price; seller shares savings below target and cost overruns up to ceiling
Use FFP when scope is clear; CR when scope is uncertain; T&M for short-term, undefined work

learning_objectives: Prepare RFI, RFP, and RFQ documents
Design vendor evaluation criteria and scoring systems
Conduct a fair and transparent vendor selection process

lecture_notes.introduction: The solicitation process must be structured to attract qualified vendors and enable objective evaluation.
key_concepts: RFI (Request for Information): market research, no commitment
RFP (Request for Proposal): detailed requirements, vendors propose solutions
RFQ (Request for Quotation): defined specifications, vendors provide prices
Evaluation criteria: technical approach, experience, cost, schedule, risk
Weighted scoring model: assign weights to criteria, score proposals, calculate weighted totals
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluating three vendor proposals using a weighted scoring model for an IT infrastructure project

review.value: Prevents cost from dominating; technical quality, experience, and risk must be evaluated alongside price
RFP for complex solutions where approach matters; RFQ for commodities where price is the primary differentiator

learning_objectives: Apply negotiation strategies for contract terms and conditions
Manage vendor performance using KPIs and SLAs
Handle contract changes through formal change control
readings: Fisher & Ury, Getting to Yes, Ch. 2-3

lecture_notes.introduction: The contract is signed, but the work has just begun. Contract administration ensures the vendor delivers what was agreed and handles inevitable changes.
key_concepts: Negotiation preparation: BATNA, reservation price, target price, zone of possible agreement
Key contract terms: scope, payment schedule, change process, penalties, termination, IP ownership
KPIs and SLAs: measurable performance standards with consequences for non-compliance
Change management: formal process for contract modifications with cost and schedule impacts
Vendor relationship management: partnership vs. adversarial approaches
lecture_notes.demonstration: Simulated contract negotiation between a buyer and seller with conflicting priorities

review.value: Zone of Possible Agreement: the range between buyer's maximum and seller's minimum where a deal is possible
Service Level Agreement defines measurable performance expectations; must include measurement method and consequences

learning_objectives: Understand key legal concepts in contracts
readings: Construction Law Handbook (selected chapters on dispute resolution)

lecture_notes.introduction: Procurement introduces risks unique to external relationships. Legal awareness protects both the project and the organization.
key_concepts: Procurement risks: vendor failure, scope disagreements, quality issues, dependency, IP disputes
Risk allocation: contract terms should place risk with the party best able to manage it
Key legal concepts: privity of contract, indemnification, limitation of liability, warranties
Dispute resolution: negotiation, mediation, arbitration, litigation (escalation order)
Force majeure: events beyond control that excuse performance
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing a contract dispute case study and recommending a resolution approach

review.value: Do not push all risk to the vendor; they will either add contingency (increasing price) or cut corners (reducing quality)
Mediation is non-binding facilitated negotiation; arbitration produces a binding decision; both are faster and cheaper than litigation

learning_objectives: Capture procurement lessons learned for organizational knowledge

lecture_notes.introduction: Proper contract closure protects the organization and ensures all obligations are met before final payment.
key_concepts: Contract closure checklist: all deliverables accepted, all invoices paid, warranties documented
Final vendor performance evaluation: scorecard across quality, schedule, cost, communication
Formal acceptance: signed documentation that all contract terms have been fulfilled
Open items resolution: punch lists, warranty period commencement, knowledge transfer
Procurement lessons learned: vendor selection effectiveness, contract type suitability, relationship management
lecture_notes.demonstration: Walking through a contract closure process for a completed construction subcontract

review.value: Written acceptance by the authorized buyer representative; triggers final payment and warranty period start
Objective scorecard informs future vendor selection; shared with the vendor as feedback for improvement

questions.question: A project can build a data analytics module internally for $180,000 (including $30,000 overhead) or outsource it for $150,000 (plus $15,000 in management overhead). The internal team has the required expertise. Should the project make or buy, and what factors beyond cost should be considered?
questions.answer: Cost comparison: Make = $180,000, Buy = $165,000. Buying is $15,000 cheaper. However, cost is not the only factor. Factors favoring Make: the team has expertise (core competency), no IP concerns, direct control over quality and schedule, no vendor dependency. Factors favoring Buy: frees internal resources for other work, vendor may deliver faster, transfers some risk to vendor. Recommendation depends on context: if this is a core capability the organization wants to develop, make it; if the team is already at capacity on higher-priority work, buy it.

rubric.key: Correct cost comparison for both options

questions.question: Under a CPIF contract, the target cost is $100,000, target fee is $10,000, sharing ratio is 80/20 (buyer/seller), and the ceiling price is $120,000. If actual costs are $90,000, calculate the seller's total payment.
questions.answer: Cost underrun = $100,000 - $90,000 = $10,000. Seller's share of savings = 20% x $10,000 = $2,000. Adjusted fee = $10,000 + $2,000 = $12,000. Total payment to seller = $90,000 (actual cost) + $12,000 (adjusted fee) = $102,000. This is below the ceiling price of $120,000, so the ceiling does not apply.

rubric.key: Correct fee adjustment using sharing ratio
Correct total payment and ceiling check

quizzes.topic: Contract Administration, Risk, and Legal Concepts

questions.question: A vendor is consistently delivering work 5 days late but quality meets all specifications. The contract has a liquidated damages clause of $500/day for late delivery. What should the project manager do?
questions.answer: The PM should: (1) Document each late delivery with dates and evidence, (2) Formally notify the vendor of the contract breach citing the specific SLA terms, (3) Enforce the liquidated damages clause for each late delivery ($2,500 per occurrence), (4) Request a corrective action plan from the vendor explaining root cause and remediation, (5) Schedule a performance review meeting to discuss the pattern, (6) If the pattern continues after corrective action, consider escalating to contract termination provisions. The PM should not waive the damages without formal amendment, as this could set a precedent.

rubric.key: Documents the breach and formally notifies vendor
Requests corrective action and plans escalation

questions.question: Explain why mediation is generally preferable to arbitration or litigation for resolving project contract disputes.
questions.answer: Mediation is preferable because: (1) Cost: mediation is significantly cheaper than arbitration or litigation (no extended discovery, shorter proceedings), (2) Speed: mediation can resolve disputes in days vs. months or years, (3) Relationship preservation: mediation is collaborative, preserving the working relationship needed to finish the project, (4) Control: parties control the outcome (unlike arbitration where a third party decides), (5) Confidentiality: mediation is private, avoiding public exposure of disputes, (6) Flexibility: creative solutions are possible that a court or arbitrator could not order. Mediation should be attempted first; if it fails, arbitration is the next step before litigation.

rubric.key: Identifies at least 3 advantages of mediation
Correctly contrasts with arbitration and litigation
Notes relationship preservation as key project factor

major_assignments.title: Procurement Strategy and Contract Package
major_assignments.description: Develop a complete procurement package for a major project procurement including make-or-buy analysis, RFP, evaluation criteria, contract type selection with justification, and contract administration plan.
objectives: Conduct a make-or-buy analysis with quantitative and qualitative factors
Write a professional RFP with clear requirements and evaluation criteria
Select and justify the appropriate contract type
Design a contract administration plan with KPIs and change control
deliverables: Complete RFP document with SOW, evaluation criteria, and submission requirements
Contract type selection paper with risk analysis justification (2-3 pages)
Vendor evaluation scorecard with weighted criteria
Contract administration plan with KPIs, SLAs, and change control process

criteria: Quantitative comparison with realistic cost data
SOW is clear, complete, and achievable
Evaluation criteria are relevant and weighted
RFP format is professional and follows best practices
Appropriate contract type selected for the procurement
KPIs and SLAs are measurable and achievable
Change control process is clearly defined

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on procurement planning, contract types, and administration

midterm_examination.description: Make-or-buy, contract types, and solicitation processes

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all procurement management topics

class_participation.description: Participation in negotiation simulations, vendor evaluations, and case studies

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all sessions. Negotiation simulations require in-person participation.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 5 days past the due date.
course_policies.collaboration: Negotiation simulations are collaborative. The procurement package assignment is individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work must be original. RFP content must not be copied from real organizational documents without permission and attribution.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Spreadsheet application for cost analysis and vendor evaluation scoring. Word processor for RFP and contract documents.
